claims.claim: Anthropic turns Claude into a persistent Slack teammate for whole teams
claims.summary_span: Anthropic turns Claude into a persistent Slack teammate for whole teams
claims.source_span: Anthropic launched Claude Tag, a Slack-native way to delegate work to Claude as if it were a teammate... Claude joins as a team member, with access to selected channels and chosen tools/data/codebases... Claude Tag is 'Claude Code made multiplayer, async, and proactive across your whole team'
claims.note: Source confirms Claude Tag is a Slack-native, team-wide, persistent agent product from Anthropic.

claims.claim: Anthropic's Claude Tag ships as a Slack-native agent that monitors channels, tags colleagues, and acts without being prompted.
claims.summary_span: Anthropic's Claude Tag ships as a Slack-native agent that monitors channels, tags colleagues, and acts without being prompted.
claims.source_span: Tag can tag in coworkers who own related code... Tag in ambient behavior mode: responds to channels without being tagged... watches for thresholds to trigger and then attempts to fix if something broke
claims.note: Source confirms channel monitoring, tagging colleagues, and proactive/unprompted behavior.

claims.claim: Anthropic's own team says it now merges 65% of product pull requests
claims.summary_span: Anthropic's own team says it now merges 65% of product pull requests
claims.source_span: Anthropic employees claimed internal metrics of 65% of code written / 65% of product PRs merged @ClaudeDevs, @_catwu
claims.note: Source confirms Anthropic's internal team reported the 65% of product PRs metric.

claims.claim: a vendor-supplied figure worth treating with scepticism
claims.summary_span: a vendor-supplied figure worth treating with scepticism
claims.source_span: Opinions / interpretations... 'This has completely changed how I work' and 'feels less like using a tool and more like managing a team' are user-experience judgments from Anthropic... 'writes 65% of our product team's code' / 'merges 65% of product PRs' depending on the speaker, which likely reflects different denominators and should not be treated as identical without clarification
claims.note: Source itself flags the 65% figure as internally reported and notes ambiguity in the metric, supporting the scepticism hedge.

claims.claim: The async, multiplayer pattern is real
claims.summary_span: The async, multiplayer pattern is real
claims.source_span: Claude Tag is 'Claude Code made multiplayer, async, and proactive across your whole team'... Work mode: asynchronous delegation via tagging
claims.note: Source confirms the async, multiplayer nature of Claude Tag as a real product characteristic.

claims.claim: Bank of England finds popular explainability tools mislead on correlated data
claims.summary_span: Bank of England finds popular explainability tools mislead on correlated data
claims.source_span: once correlation is introduced, ranking performance worsens substantially... feature importance explanations from a single model run can be highly misleading
claims.note: The source is published on Bank Underground (Bank of England's research blog) and confirms explainability tools mislead when features are correlated; 'Bank of England' is a supported paraphrase of the publisher.

claims.claim: The Bank of England's research blog ran 500 simulations
claims.summary_span: The Bank of England's research blog ran 500 simulations
claims.source_span: This was repeated 500 times to see how stable the results are.
claims.note: Source confirms 500 repeated simulation runs; Bank Underground is the Bank of England's research blog.

claims.claim: SHAP, LIME, and similar feature attribution methods rank inputs correctly only when features are independent
claims.summary_span: SHAP, LIME, and similar feature attribution methods rank inputs correctly only when features are independent
claims.source_span: Explainability methods are reliable only when features are independent, but their performance deteriorates sharply once features become even mildly correlated
claims.note: Source explicitly states methods are reliable only under independence; SHAP and LIME are named in the source as examples of these methods.

claims.claim: Rank agreement drops to between 0.3 and 0.8
claims.summary_span: rank agreement drops to between 0.3 and 0.8
claims.source_span: median rank agreement coefficients fall (typically to between 0.3 and 0.8)
claims.note: Exact range matches the source verbatim.

claims.claim: Adding mild correlation makes explanations actively misleading
claims.summary_span: making explanations actively misleading
claims.source_span: feature importance explanations from a single model run can be highly misleading
claims.note: Source supports the claim that correlated features cause misleading explanations; 'actively misleading' is a fair paraphrase of 'highly misleading'.

claims.claim: Simulating real deployments predicts model safety before release
claims.summary_span: Simulating real deployments predicts model safety before release
claims.source_span: We've now started using a method for simulating model deployments before they happen, which adds a complementary signal: a deployment-like preview of how a candidate model may behave before it reaches users.
claims.note: The headline accurately paraphrases the deployment simulation method's purpose of predicting safety behaviour before release.

claims.claim: Replaying anonymised production conversations with a candidate model before release
claims.summary_span: Replaying anonymised production conversations with a candidate model before release
claims.source_span: We do so by replaying previous conversations in a privacy-preserving manner with a new candidate model.
claims.note: 'Anonymised' is a reasonable plain-English paraphrase of 'privacy-preserving manner'.

claims.claim: a new technique predicted the direction of safety-behaviour changes 92% of the time
claims.summary_span: a new technique predicted the direction of safety-behaviour changes 92% of the time
claims.source_span: deployment simulation predicted the direction of change 92% of the time
claims.note: The 92% figure and 'direction of change' match the source exactly.

claims.claim: against 54% for a standard prompt-based baseline
claims.summary_span: against 54% for a standard prompt-based baseline
claims.source_span: compared with 54% for a baseline built from challenging prompts
claims.note: 'Standard prompt-based baseline' is a fair paraphrase of 'a baseline built from challenging prompts'.

claims.claim: No public code yet.
claims.summary_span: No public code yet.
claims.source_span: As we make the pipeline easier to run, we expect it to play a larger role in the future model development process.
claims.note: The source never mentions public code release; the body's claim is consistent with the source's framing of the pipeline as still being developed internally.

claims.claim: OpenAI builds its own inference chip to reduce reliance on Nvidia
claims.summary_span: OpenAI builds its own inference chip to reduce reliance on Nvidia
claims.note: Source excerpt is empty; no source material exists to confirm or deny this claim.

claims.claim: Jalapeño is a custom chip designed specifically for running large language models at scale

claims.claim: No performance benchmarks or deployment timeline are published yet
claims.summary_span: No performance benchmarks or deployment timeline are published yet

claims.claim: A careful synthesis of scaling laws sharpens compute allocation decisions
claims.summary_span: A careful synthesis of scaling laws sharpens compute allocation decisions
claims.source_span: We can view scaling laws as a framework for describing the relationship between compute, loss, model size and data; at its core, it is about how to allocate precious compute optimally between $N$ and $D$.
claims.note: The source is a synthesis of scaling-law research focused on compute allocation, supporting the headline as a paraphrase.

claims.claim: Lilian Weng's synthesis distils three decades of scaling-law research into one reference.
claims.summary_span: Lilian Weng's synthesis distils three decades of scaling-law research into one reference.
claims.note: The source excerpt never names the author, so the attribution to Lilian Weng and the 'three decades' characterisation cannot be confirmed or denied from this excerpt.

claims.claim: Training loss falls predictably as a power law across model size, data, and compute.
claims.summary_span: The core insight: training loss falls predictably as a power law across model size, data, and compute.
claims.source_span: the training loss $L$ decreases predictably as we scale up model size $N$, dataset size $D$, and compute $C$, following a power-law curve
claims.note: Direct paraphrase of the source's core finding; verdict is supported.

claims.claim: No new empirical findings; the value is calibration.
claims.summary_span: No new empirical findings; the value is calibration.
claims.note: The source excerpt does not characterise the piece as containing no new empirical findings; this editorial framing cannot be verified from the excerpt.

claims.claim: One command turns an ETF holding list into an institutional crowding scan
claims.summary_span: One command turns an ETF holding list into an institutional crowding scan
claims.source_span: A user simply asks the Agent to run sector-smart-money-scan on SOXX and show top holdings, 13F crowding, NVDA duplicate exposure, and ninety-day performance.
claims.note: The source confirms a single prompt/command produces a structured crowding scan from ETF holdings and 13F data.

claims.claim: LLMQuant's sector scan wires Claude Code and GPT Codex to live ETF holdings, 13F filings, and price data
claims.summary_span: LLMQuant's sector scan wires Claude Code and GPT Codex to live ETF holdings, 13F filings, and price data
claims.source_span: How Claude Code and GPT Codex, wired into structured ETF, 13F, and price data, turn a tedious research chore into a single, reviewable page
claims.note: Source confirms Claude Code and GPT Codex are wired into ETF, 13F, and price data; 'live' is a minor paraphrase but not contradicted.

claims.claim: collapsing a multi-step research chore into a single prompt
claims.summary_span: collapsing a multi-step research chore into a single prompt
claims.source_span: turn a tedious research chore into a single, reviewable page ... The Skill compresses that entire sequence into a single conversation.
claims.note: Source confirms the multi-step chore is compressed into a single interaction.

claims.claim: The demo runs on SOXX
claims.summary_span: The demo runs on SOXX
claims.source_span: A user simply asks the Agent to run sector-smart-money-scan on SOXX
claims.note: Source confirms SOXX is the target of the demo scan.

claims.claim: surfacing 56.3% top-ten concentration
claims.summary_span: surfacing 56.3% top-ten concentration
claims.source_span: shows top ten concentration of 56.3 percent
claims.note: Exact figure matches the source.

claims.claim: 779 institutional holders for NVDA alone
claims.summary_span: 779 institutional holders for NVDA alone
claims.source_span: NVDA sits at an 8.3 percent weight, appears in 779 institutional 13F filings
claims.note: Exact figure matches the source.

claims.claim: Single-source, vendor-written; verify the data pipeline before using output in a live position.
claims.summary_span: Single-source, vendor-written; verify the data pipeline before using output in a live position.
claims.note: The source excerpt does not characterise the piece as 'single-source' or 'vendor-written'; this trust-flag qualifier cannot be confirmed or denied from the excerpt.

claims.claim: Local models now run agentic coding at about 75% of frontier accuracy and speed
claims.summary_span: Local models now run agentic coding at about 75% of frontier accuracy and speed
claims.source_span: I've finally been able to do agentic coding locally and have loops work at about ~75% the accuracy/speed of frontier models
claims.note: Source directly states ~75% accuracy/speed for agentic coding loops, matching the headline.

claims.claim: Vicki Boykis's practitioner survey across llama.cpp, Ollama, and LM Studio on an M2 Mac
claims.summary_span: Vicki Boykis's practitioner survey across llama.cpp, Ollama, and LM Studio on an M2 Mac
claims.note: The source never names the author; 'Vicki Boykis' cannot be confirmed or denied from this excerpt.

claims.claim: local agentic coding loops running at roughly 75% the accuracy and speed of frontier models
claims.summary_span: local agentic coding loops running at roughly 75% the accuracy and speed of frontier models
claims.source_span: I've finally been able to do agentic coding locally and have loops work at about ~75% the accuracy/speed of frontier models
claims.note: Direct match to source figure; 'roughly' is an acceptable paraphrase of '~'.

claims.claim: Self-reported, no controlled benchmark
claims.summary_span: Self-reported, no controlled benchmark
claims.source_span: I have no concrete scientific evidence of this - my own personal vibe metric of "is a model good enough"
claims.note: Source explicitly disclaims scientific evidence and describes a personal vibe metric, supporting the 'self-reported, no controlled benchmark' characterisation.

claims.claim: Pull her Pi-plus-LM-Studio Docker config and run it against your coding task set before renewing a cloud inference subscription
claims.summary_span: Pull her Pi-plus-LM-Studio Docker config
claims.source_span: For my local setup, I'm currently using Pi as the agent harness and LM Studio as the inference server... I run every Pi session in a Docker container
claims.note: Source confirms Pi + LM Studio + Docker config is shared; 'Pi-plus-LM-Studio Docker config' is a fair compression of the described setup.

claims.claim: NVIDIA's fine-tuning library cuts mixture-of-experts training time by 3.5x
claims.summary_span: NVIDIA's fine-tuning library cuts mixture-of-experts training time by 3.5x
claims.source_span: The payoff is 3.4-3.7x higher training throughput and 29-32% less GPU memory on fine-tuning MoE models than native Transformers v5
claims.note: 3.5x is a reasonable midpoint rounding of the 3.4–3.7x range reported in the source; 'training time' vs 'training throughput' is an acceptable paraphrase inversion.

claims.claim: NVIDIA's NeMo AutoModel slots into the standard Hugging Face from_pretrained() call via a single import change
claims.summary_span: NVIDIA's NeMo AutoModel slots into the standard Hugging Face from_pretrained() call via a single import change
claims.source_span: using the same from_pretrained() API: a single import line, with no other code changes
claims.note: 'Hugging Face from_pretrained()' is a correct plain-English identification of the API described in the source; single import change is verbatim.

claims.claim: NeMo AutoModel delivers 3.4–3.7x faster training throughput
claims.summary_span: delivering 3.4–3.7x faster training throughput
claims.source_span: The payoff is 3.4-3.7x higher training throughput
claims.note: Exact range matches the source.

claims.claim: NeMo AutoModel delivers roughly 30% less GPU memory on mixture-of-experts models
claims.summary_span: roughly 30% less GPU memory on mixture-of-experts models
claims.source_span: 29-32% less GPU memory on fine-tuning MoE models
claims.note: 'Roughly 30%' is a valid rounding of the 29–32% range.

claims.claim: Benchmarks are vendor-supplied
claims.summary_span: Benchmarks are vendor-supplied
claims.source_span: We evaluated NeMo AutoModel in two regimes
claims.note: The source is an NVIDIA blog reporting NVIDIA's own measurements, making 'vendor-supplied' an accurate trust qualifier.

claims.claim: A lightweight text-reader covers 50 languages, no GPU required
claims.summary_span: A lightweight text-reader covers 50 languages, no GPU required
claims.source_span: The medium and small tiers support 50 languages, including Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, English, Japanese, and 46 Latin-script languages. ... PP-OCRv6 OCR with ONNX Runtime CPU backend
claims.note: 50-language coverage is confirmed; CPU-only operation is confirmed by the ONNX Runtime CPU backend title and the lightweight framing; 'text-reader' is a plain-English paraphrase of OCR.

claims.claim: PP-OCRv6 lands on Hugging Face with three model tiers covering 50 languages
claims.summary_span: PP-OCRv6 lands on Hugging Face with three model tiers covering 50 languages
claims.source_span: The model family scales from 1.5M to 34.5M parameters, with three tiers: tiny, small, and medium. The medium and small tiers support 50 languages
claims.note: Three tiers and 50-language support are confirmed; the body's generalisation that the family 'covers 50 languages' is a minor compression of the source's 'medium and small tiers support 50 languages', not a material error.

claims.claim: the medium tier hits 83.2% recognition accuracy on vendor-supplied benchmarks
claims.summary_span: the medium tier hits 83.2% recognition accuracy on vendor-supplied benchmarks
claims.source_span: On PaddleOCR's official in-house multi-scenario OCR benchmarks, PP-OCRv6_medium reaches 86.2% detection Hmean and 83.2% recognition accuracy.
claims.note: The 83.2% recognition accuracy figure is confirmed; 'vendor-supplied benchmarks' correctly flags the in-house nature of the benchmark.

claims.claim: It runs via PaddleOCR, Transformers, or ONNX Runtime with no GPU required
claims.summary_span: It runs via PaddleOCR, Transformers, or ONNX Runtime with no GPU required
claims.source_span: PP-OCRv6 can be used with multiple inference backends through PaddleOCR. ... engine="transformers" ... engine="onnxruntime" ... PP-OCRv6 OCR with ONNX Runtime CPU backend
claims.note: All three backends are confirmed in the source; CPU-only operation is confirmed by the ONNX Runtime CPU backend reference.
